narrative: As a Hispanic male applying to law school, your background and academic record were carefully evaluated by our admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try. The model assessed key aspects of your profile, including your undergraduate GPA of 2.90, LSAT score of 35, family income in the middle bracket, and your rankings in the bottom decile for grades in both your first and third years of school, along with your plan to attend full-time. It calculated an 82.1% probability that you would fail the bar exam, a risk level that led to the rejection of your application to protect the program's overall success rates. The decision hinged primarily on three factors, ranked by their influence on this specific prediction for you. Most significantly, your third-year school decile ranking in the bottom tenth pushed the model strongly toward predicting bar failure, as it signals challenges in sustaining strong performance over time compared to typical students who rank around the middle of their class. Your first-year decile, also in the bottom tenth well below the average fifth or sixth decile, reinforced this concern by highlighting early academic struggles that often correlate with bar exam difficulties. On a more positive note, your LSAT score of 35 pulled the prediction somewhat toward passing, though not enough to offset the other indicators, especially since it's slightly below the typical 36.7 average. While your middle-income background and full-time status align with most applicants—over 92% of whom are full-time—these didn't sway the outcome much here. In comparison to typical students who pass the bar, your lower decile rankings stand out as bigger hurdles than your LSAT provides uplift. This isn't a judgment on your potential or resilience as a Hispanic man pursuing law, but a data-driven assessment to ensure admitted students are set up for bar success. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, improving your GPA through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and gathering strong letters highlighting your growth and dedication. Reapplying after addressing these core areas could significantly shift the model's view in your favor.
